m_Item1.QuestionText: What is Composition in C++?

Options.OptionText: A way to implement multiple inheritance
A design principle where a class contains objects of other classes
A technique to override base class methods
A method to create new classes from existing ones

m_Item1.Explanation: Composition involves one class being composed of one or more objects of other classes, establishing a "has-a" relationship.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which relationship best describes Composition?
m_Item1.Explanation: Composition implies that a class has another class as a part of its own structure.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Composition, how are the lifetimes of the objects managed?

Options.OptionText: The containing object manages the lifetime of the contained objects.
The contained objects manage the lifetime of the containing object.
Both objects manage their own lifetimes independently.
The lifetimes are managed automatically by the compiler.

m_Item1.Explanation: The containing object in Composition is responsible for managing the lifetime of the objects it contains.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens when the containing object in Composition is destroyed?

Options.OptionText: The contained objects are also destroyed automatically.
The contained objects become inaccessible but remain in memory.
The contained objects are copied to another location in memory.
The contained objects become global objects.

m_Item1.Explanation: Destruction of the containing object in Composition results in the automatic destruction of the contained objects.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which word is used to denote Composition in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: The has relationship is often used to denote Composition in class relationships.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following describes Aggregation in C++?

Options.OptionText: A design principle where a class is derived from multiple base classes.
A design principle where a class contains a pointer to an object of another class.
A design principle where a class is used to create objects of another class.
A design principle where a class inherits from its own members.

m_Item1.Explanation: Aggregation denotes a relationship where one class has a pointer or reference to another class, forming a "has-a" relationship.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Aggregation, how are the lifetimes of the objects managed?

Options.OptionText: The containing object manages the lifetime of the contained objects.
The contained objects manage the lifetime of the containing object.
Both objects manage their own lifetimes independently.
The lifetimes are managed automatically by the compiler.

m_Item1.Explanation: In Aggregation, both objects manage their own lifetimes independently of each other.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which relationship best describes Aggregation?
m_Item1.Explanation: Aggregation is often described as a "has-a" relationship, indicating ownership or containment.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Aggregation, what happens when the containing object is destroyed?

Options.OptionText: The contained objects are also destroyed automatically.
The contained objects become inaccessible but remain in memory.
The contained objects are copied to another location in memory.
The contained objects become global objects.

m_Item1.Explanation: Destruction of the containing object in Aggregation does not automatically destroy the contained objects, which may remain in memory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true about Composition and Aggregation?

Options.OptionText: Composition and Aggregation are identical concepts.
Aggregation is a specialized form of Composition.
Composition is a specialized form of Aggregation.
Composition and Aggregation are unrelated concepts.

m_Item1.Explanation: Composition and Aggregation are related concepts, with Composition being a stricter form where the contained objects are part of the containing object's lifecycle.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of Composition in C++?

Options.OptionText: A Car class with a Driver class as a member.
A Teacher class having a pointer to a Course class.
A Library class containing multiple Book objects.
A Company class inheriting from an Employee class.

m_Item1.Explanation: Composition is exemplified by a class (e.g., Library) containing objects of another class (e.g., Book).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of Aggregation in C++?

Options.OptionText: A House class containing Room objects.
A Customer class inheriting from a Person class.
A University class having a Student class as a member.
A Bank class using a Transaction class.

m_Item1.Explanation: Aggregation is exemplified by a class (e.g., House) having a pointer or reference to objects of another class (e.g., Room).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which type of relationship is more loosely coupled: Composition or Aggregation?
m_Item1.Explanation: Aggregation is generally more loosely coupled as the objects involved can exist independently of each other.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C++, can a class have both Composition and Aggregation relationships with other classes?

Options.OptionText: Yes, and they can be used interchangeably.
No, a class can only have one type of relationship.
Yes, but they must be clearly distinguished in the class definition.
No, Comosition and Aggregation are mutually exclusive.

m_Item1.Explanation: It is possible for a class in C++ to have both Composition and Aggregation relationships with other classes, but it is important to clearly differentiate between them.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a disadvantage of using Composition over Aggregation?
m_Item1.Explanation: Composition can lead to tighter coupling between classes compared to Aggregation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which design principle promotes better code organization and modularity: Composition or Aggregation?

Options.OptionText: Both promote similar levels of organization.

m_Item1.Explanation: Composition often promotes better code organization and modularity by encapsulating functionality within objects.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does containment mean in object-oriented programming?

Options.OptionText: Using functions outside of a class
Including one class as a member in another class

m_Item1.Explanation: Containment in OOP refers to including one class as a member object within another class, often called the 'has-a' relationship.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of containment?
m_Item1.Explanation: Containment is realized by including an instance of another class as a member of the containing class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Containment is also known as:
m_Item1.Explanation: Containment is often referred to as composition, where a class is composed of objects of other classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What keyword is crucial for the implementation of containment in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'class' keyword is used to define a new class, and inside it, you can include member objects from other classes to achieve containment.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement best describes the relationship 'class Car { Engine engine; };'?

Options.OptionText: Car is a type of Engine
Engine is a derived class of Car

m_Item1.Explanation: This represents a 'has-a' relationship, where the class Car contains an object of the class Engine.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a key use of containment in C++?

Options.OptionText: To hold references and pointers to base classes
To create complex objects from simpler ones

m_Item1.Explanation: Containment is used to build complex objects by using objects of other classes as members, leveraging existing functionalities.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does containment differ from inheritance?

Options.OptionText: Containment creates a 'is-a' relationship, while inheritance creates 'has-a'
Containment increases memory usage, while inheritance doesn't
Containment involves using another class's objects, whereas inheritance involves deriving a new class
Containment uses virtual functions, inheritance does not

m_Item1.Explanation: Containment uses instances of other classes within a class, creating a 'has-a' relationship, unlike inheritance which creates an 'is-a' relationship.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which C++ concept is most closely related to containment?
m_Item1.Explanation: Containment is very similar to object composition where a class is composed of other classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If class A contains objects of class B, how is the relationship often described?

Options.OptionText: A is an ancestor of B
B is an interface for A

m_Item1.Explanation: When a class A contains objects of class B, it's often described as A using B.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary advantage of using containment?

Options.OptionText: Increases the size of compiled binaries

m_Item1.Explanation: Containment allows reusability and modularity by constructing classes from existing classes, promoting organized design.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which feature is unnecessary for implementing containment?
m_Item1.Explanation: Pointer arithmetic is unrelated to the implementation of containment, which focuses on object relationships.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When using containment, how are the lifetimes of member objects typically managed?

Options.OptionText: Member objects' lifetimes are not related to the containing object
They are created and destroyed independently
They are automatically handled by the containing object's lifetime
They require manual memory management always

m_Item1.Explanation: In containment, member objects are usually created and destroyed along with the containing object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the relationship between containment and encapsulation?

Options.OptionText: Containment is a part of encapsulation
Encapsulation is a type of containment

m_Item1.Explanation: Containment can be considered as part of encapsulation since it involves wrapping data and functionality under a unified class interface.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which pattern often uses containment in its implementation?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Strategy Pattern often uses containment to hold references to implementations of interchangeable algorithms.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C++, how is access to contained objects typically managed?

Options.OptionText: Through member functions of the containing class

m_Item1.Explanation: Access to contained objects is typically managed using member functions of the containing class to maintain encapsulation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does changing a contained object affect the containing object?

Options.OptionText: Depends on how it's integrated in the containing class

m_Item1.Explanation: The effect on the containing object depends on the role of the contained object within the class and the design of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which method is often necessary when managing resources in a contained class?
m_Item1.Explanation: Destructors, copy constructors, and assignment operators are important for proper resource management when dealing with contained objects.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why might reference members be used in a class instead of object containment?
m_Item1.Explanation: Using references can avoid the overhead of copying large objects, which can be beneficial for performance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does containment help in achieving loose coupling?
m_Item1.Explanation: Containment allows for interchangeable components, fostering loose coupling by reducing dependencies.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of containment?
m_Item1.Explanation: Containment offers benefits in architecture and manageability, but does not inherently guarantee faster execution speed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C++, what does 'Association' typically refer to?

Options.OptionText: A type of relationship between classes

m_Item1.Explanation: Association in C++ is a concept that defines a relationship between two classes, where one class uses or has a reference to another class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following best describes 'Aggregation' in C++ association?

Options.OptionText: A weak association relationship where lifecycle is independent
A strong association relationship with shared lifecycle
A many-to-many relationship with dependent lifecycles
A subtype of association with only public members

m_Item1.Explanation: Aggregation is a form of association with a 'whole-part' relationship, where the lifecycle of the contained object is not dictated by the containing object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In terms of association, what is a 'Composition' in C++?

Options.OptionText: A strong association with dependent lifecycles

m_Item1.Explanation: Composition is a form of association where contained objects’ lifecycle is managed by the containing object; when the container is destroyed, so are its contents.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a type of association in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: Generalization is often related to inheritance hierarchies rather than associations which focus on relationships other than inheritance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which UML relationship implies association if both classes need to be aware of each other?
m_Item1.Explanation: Bidirectional association means both classes are aware of each other and can interact directly with each other in UML design.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What symbol is used to denote composition in UML diagrams?
m_Item1.Explanation: Composition is represented by a filled diamond at the end of the association line in UML diagrams.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In aggregation, can the associated class exist independently of the containing class?

Options.OptionText: Only if it is a friend class
Only if both classes are abstract

m_Item1.Explanation: In aggregation, the associated class can exist independently of the containing class and does not rely on the container for its lifecycle.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When implementing composition in C++, which of the following relationships is assumed?

Options.OptionText: Implicit direct access with global variables
Strong ownership with contained object's lifecycle tied to owner

m_Item1.Explanation: Composition implies a strong lifecycle bond where the container ‘owns’ the objects and manages their lifecycle.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can associations be implemented in C++?

Options.OptionText: Through pointers or references to another class

m_Item1.Explanation: Associations in C++ can be implemented using pointers or references, allowing one class to use or communicate with another.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following signifies a one-to-many association in C++?

Options.OptionText: By having multiple pointers to objects of another class

m_Item1.Explanation: A one-to-many relationship is represented by one class holding references or pointers to multiple instances of another class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a unidirectional association, which class knows about the other in C++?

Options.OptionText: Both classes know about each other
Only the client class is aware of the server class
Only the server class is aware of the client class
Neither class knows about each other

m_Item1.Explanation: In a unidirectional association, the client class has knowledge of and can interact with the server class, but not vice versa.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which C++ feature closely resembles the concept of association in object-oriented analysis?
m_Item1.Explanation: Friend classes can access each other’s private and protected members, resembling close associations in object-oriented design.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is not related to association directly but indicates access in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'extern' keyword is used for indicating linkage, not directly related to associations and access specifications like public, private, or protected.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following can impact an association relationship in terms of class design?

Options.OptionText: The position of the class in memory
The encapsulation level of class members

m_Item1.Explanation: Encapsulation defines access control and can close or open association pathways, impacting how associations are designed and implemented.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What can a destructor in C++ jeopardize in terms of association?

Options.OptionText: The modular design of the software
The lifecycle of associated objects in a composition
The polymorphic behavior of inherited objects
The external linkage of global variables

m_Item1.Explanation: In composition, when a container object is destroyed, its destructor deletes all composed objects, impacting their lifecycle.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If Class A is associated with Class B, how can this association be represented in code?

Options.OptionText: By including a reference or pointer to Class B in Class A

m_Item1.Explanation: Associations are commonly represented in code by having pointers or references of one class in another, suggesting a relationship.

m_Item1.QuestionText: For ensuring that one object should not outlive its owning object, which association type should be used?
m_Item1.Explanation: Composition tightly binds the lifetimes of contained objects to their container, ensuring they are deleted together, preventing outliving.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why are associations significant in object-oriented programming?

Options.OptionText: They formalize relationships and interactions between classes
They enable inline functions without header modifications

m_Item1.Explanation: Associations detail how objects and classes interact, playing a critical role in modeling real-world relationships and operations in software.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a composition relationship, who is responsible for deleting the owned objects?
m_Item1.Explanation: In composition, the owner class manages the lifecycle of its owned objects and is responsible for their deletion to avoid resource leaks.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When does aggregation become indistinguishable from an association?

Options.OptionText: When the container uses shared_ptr to manage objects
When static data members are involved

m_Item1.Explanation: Aggregation is a form of association; it becomes indistinguishable when the lifecycle of the associated objects is independent.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Composition is a stronger form of which of the following?
m_Item1.Explanation: Aggregation in C++ (commonly called as a has-a relationship), is a process in which one class defines a second class as an entity reference. It is a method of reusability of classes. In the simplest possible terms, it is when a class has an object of the other class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is meant by containment?

Options.OptionText: class contains objects of other class types as its data members
class used another class as its base class
One class is friend of another class
class contains pointer of other class as its data member.

m_Item1.Explanation: Containment is one class having object of other class a its data member. If one class has pointer of other class as its data member, it is called as Assoication.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How many ways of reusing are there in classes?
m_Item1.Explanation: Class hierarchies promote reuse in two ways. They are code sharing and interface sharing.That is  Containment and inheritance.
